methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $178M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
QuantWare, a Series B quantum computing company founded by Matt Rijlaarsdam and Alessandro Bruno, operates within a sector represented by 3 public comps (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private peers including Q-CTRL, IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, and D-Wave Quantum.

The valuation analysis relies on a single applicable method: stage-progression analysis. Using QuantWare's $178M in total raised as a base proxy, application of a 1.9x Series B step-up multiplier yields an implied valuation of $338M. This multiplier reflects typical capital step-ups observed at the Series B-to-later-stage transition. Peer-median methodology could not be applied due to insufficient clustering within narrow peer segments—while 7 same-sector quantum computing companies exist, fewer than three datapoints aligned on stage, adjacent-stage, or valuation-tier criteria necessary for robust median benchmarking.

The single-method triangulation supports a median valuation of $338M, with a range from $287M (low) to $389M (high) derived from sensitivity analysis around the stage-progression input. Key uncertainties center on the appropriateness of the 1.9x multiplier to QuantWare's specific capital efficiency and runway profile, and the lack of transaction-level or revenue-based anchors in the quantum computing peer set. A broader comparable dataset or revenue visibility would materially strengthen conviction in this range.
